Introduction to Bayesian Learning — Course Project

Bayesian Analysis
of US Spot
Bitcoin ETFs

Market structure, return inference, and tracking efficiency — through a Bayesian lens

Dataset 597 trading days · Jan 2024 – Jun 2026
Methods Beta-Binomial · Normal-InvGamma · MCMC
Context

The same asset. Six wrappers. One day.

IBITBlackRock iSharesFee 0.25% · AUM rank #1
FBTCFidelityFee 0.25% · AUM rank #2
BITBBitwiseFee 0.20% · AUM rank #3
ARKBARK 21SharesFee 0.21% · AUM rank #4
HODLVanEckFee 0.20% · AUM rank #5
BTCOInvesco GalaxyFee 0.25% · AUM rank #6

All six launched on January 11, 2024. All hold only spot Bitcoin. Any differences in market behaviour must come from fee, brand, or institutional backing — making this an unusually clean natural experiment for Bayesian inference.

Data

597 trading days of prices and volumes

VARIABLES CONSTRUCTED

  • Daily log-return: r_t = log(P_t / P_{t-1})
  • Daily volume share: s_{k,t} = vol_k / Σvol
  • Daily tracking error: TE_{k,t} = r_ETF − r_BTC

SAMPLE SPLIT

  • Year 1 Jan 2024 – Jan 2025 · 250 days
  • Year 2+ Jan 2025 – Jun 2026 · 347 days

Source: Yahoo Finance via quantmod · Return correlation across all pairs: 0.9995–0.9998

Fig 1 · Normalised prices — all ETFs + BTC (base 100 at launch)

Fig 2 · Daily volume shares — convergence on IBIT by 2025

Methodology

Five questions, four Bayesian tools

BETA-BINOMIAL CONJUGACY

Proportion inference for market dominance. Prior: Beta(α₁,α₂) → closed-form posterior.

NORMAL-INVGAMMA CONJUGATE

Return and variance inference. Marginal posterior follows scaled t-distribution analytically.

IMPROPER JEFFREYS PRIOR

Shows Bayesian ≡ frequentist t-test numerically. Scaled inv-χ² posterior for σ².

BAYESIAN REGRESSION (MCMC)

brms/Stan HMC sampler. Priors on β informed by ETF mechanics. Convergence via R-hat + ESS.

Prior × Likelihood ∝ Posterior  |  Beta-Binomial · Normal-InvGamma · Scaled inv-χ² · NUTS (HMC)
Section 2 · Beta-Binomial

Has BlackRock's dominance eroded?

IBIT captured >50% of total volume on almost every day — making any test at that threshold degenerate. We use a sharper threshold:

π = P(IBIT volume share > 70% on day t)

Estimated separately for Year 1 (pre-Jan 2025) and Year 2+ (post-Jan 2025). Prior: Uniform Beta(1,1) encoding no prior view on BlackRock's advantage.

Three priors compared
Uniform Beta(1,1)mean 0.50
Jeffreys Beta(0.5,0.5)mean 0.50
Informative Beta(9.5,3.0)mean 0.76
Posterior update (uniform prior)
π | y ~ Beta(y+1, n−y+1)

With n≥250, prior weight < 1%. Data dominates.

Section 2 · Key Finding

Dominance didn't erode. It became absolute.

Year 1 · Jan 2024 – Jan 2025 · n=250 34.8% of days IBIT crossed 70% threshold 95% HPD: (0.290, 0.408)
Year 2+ · Jan 2025 – Jun 2026 · n=347 99.7% of days IBIT crossed 70% threshold 95% HPD: (0.989, 1.000)
95% CI for π₂ − π₁: (+0.585, +0.703)
χ² = 307.3 · p < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶
Prior sensitivity check (uniform prior)
Year 1 MLE0.348
Year 1 post. mean0.349
Year 2+ MLE0.997
Year 2+ post. mean0.994
Why the test returns P = 0

Not a degenerate failure — the posteriors are so far apart that 10 million Monte Carlo draws find no sample where Year 1 > Year 2+. The gap is the finding. Posterior pdfs visualised → next slide.

Section 2 · Visualizations

Posterior pdfs and predictive distribution

Posterior Beta pdfs — Year 1 vs Year 2+. Non-overlapping distributions.

Beta-Binomial predictive — next 10 days · P(all 10 dominant) = 0.945

Prior sensitivity: even with α=100 (symmetric), prior weight is only 37%. With n≥250, the prior is irrelevant.

A three-tier market emerged

Days each ETF led among the 5 non-IBIT competitors (n=597) · Fair share = 20%

FBTC

0.0%

0.0%
Fair
20%
FBTC — undisputed #2 P(π > 0.20) = 1.000
ARKB — right at fair share P(π > 0.20) = 0.628 The only genuinely uncertain result in this section
BITB · HODL · BTCO P(π > 0.20) ≈ 0.000

Bonferroni (10 pairs): FBTC leads all *** · ARKB leads BITB/HODL/BTCO *** · BITB vs HODL vs BTCO: no evidence

Section 4 · Normal-InvGamma Conjugacy

Can we detect a positive expected return?

Y_i | μ, σ² ~ N(μ, σ²) iid
μ | σ² ~ N(μ₀, σ²/n₀)  [prior on mean]
σ² ~ InvGamma(α, β)  [prior on variance]
  • EMH prior: μ₀ = 0 — if positive returns were predictable, arbitrage eliminates them
  • n₀ = 2 imaginary prior observations → prior weight < 0.34%
  • InvGamma(0.5, 0.5) for σ² — 95% interval for σ is (44.7%, 3191%) → essentially flat over realistic values
  • Marginal posterior for μ: closed-form scaled t-distribution
Posterior mean (shrinkage formula)
E(μ|y) = [n/(n₀+n)] × ȳ + [n₀/(n₀+n)] × μ₀

With n=597 and n₀=2: data gets 99.7% weight

Posterior standard deviation
σ_n = √(2β* / (n + n₀ − 1))
Section 4 · Results

P(μ > 0) ≈ 0.63 for all ETFs — the EMH is not rejected

ETFPost. mean95% CI lower95% CI upperP(μ>0)
IBIT0.000747−0.00340+0.004890.630
FBTC0.000747−0.00340+0.004890.630
BITB0.000745−0.00340+0.004830.630
ARKB0.000746−0.00339+0.004830.630
HODL0.000753−0.00338+0.004890.632
BTCO0.000754−0.00338+0.004890.632
~19% Annualised return — but 95% CI spans −34% to +49%
±6.2% Daily standard deviation vs 0.07% mean
Signal-to-noise ratio ≈ 1/88

597 observations are nowhere near enough to pin down the mean of a process with this much variance. The 95% CI crossing zero by a wide margin is the EMH in action, not a flaw in the analysis.

Marginal posterior t-distributions for μ — all six ETFs overlap completely · EMH prior μ₀ = 0 shown as dashed line

Section 5 · Improper Priors

Bayesian and frequentist give identical results

Under the improper Jeffreys prior p(μ, σ²) ∝ 1/σ², the posterior is analytically identical to the frequentist t-distribution. The 95% credible interval equals the 95% confidence interval to five decimal places.

QuantityBayesian (improper)Frequentist
95% Interval lower−0.00183−0.00183
95% Interval upper+0.00324+0.00324
P(μ < 0) / p-value0.2930.587 (two-sided)

Interpretation differs: Bayesian gives P(hypothesis|data); frequentist gives P(data|hypothesis).

Next trading day (1-day prediction) ±6.2% 95% posterior predictive interval: (−6.13%, +6.27%)
Next 30-day mean (1-month prediction) ±1.2% 95% posterior predictive interval: (−1.08%, +1.23%)
Width ∝ √(1/m + 1/n) → s/√n as m → ∞
Prediction interval narrowing as sample size grows
Section 6 · InvGamma Variance Inference

Can we rank ETFs by tracking quality?

Daily tracking error: TE = r_ETF − r_BTC. Under improper Jeffreys prior, the posterior for σ²_TE is the scaled inverse chi-squared — same formula as in the conjugate Normal case.

ETFFeeσ_TE (bps/day)Excess drag
IBIT0.25%136.9−0.11 bps
FBTC0.25%136.5−0.11 bps
BITB0.20%135.8−0.15 bps
ARKB0.21%136.4−0.13 bps
HODL0.20%135.7−0.06 bps
BTCO0.25%135.7−0.04 bps

Bartlett K² = 0.096 · p = 0.9999 · All 15 Bonferroni pairs: "ns"

Why variances are indistinguishable

BTC trades 24/7. ETFs trade 9:30am–4pm. An overnight Bitcoin move of 2% hits all six ETFs identically at open. This common shock ≈ 136 bps/day dwarfs fee differences of ≈ 0.05 bps/day — a 2,700× ratio.

What IS informative: mean tracking error

BITB loses 0.15 bps/day above its stated fee — nearly double its expected drag. HODL and BTCO come closest to their stated expense ratio. Hidden execution costs are real but small.

Section 7 · Bayesian Regression · Main Finding

IBIT daily return = α + β × BTC return + ε

Under perfect replication: α = 0 and β = 1.000

β = 0.932
95% CI: (0.897, 0.967) R² = 0.817
99.99% P(β < 1.00 | data)
Near-certain systematic under-tracking
84.6% P(β < 0.95 | data)
Strong evidence of >5% lag
39.4% P(α > 0 | data)
No evidence of positive alpha (EMH)

R² = 0.817 means 18% of IBIT's daily variance is unrelated to Bitcoin's same-day return. Cause: overnight BTC moves appear in the next day's ETF opening price → structural β < 1.

Section 7 · Time-Split Regression

Is tracking improving as the market matures?

Periodβ (mean)95% CIP(β<1)P(β<0.95)
Year 10.928(0.878, 0.979)0.9970.796
Year 2+0.940(0.894, 0.986)0.9940.659
Full0.932(0.897, 0.967)0.99990.846
P(β improves Year 1 → Year 2+) 0.641 Moderate directional evidence of improvement,
not conclusive — gap from 1.000 remains in both periods

Volume share predictor: P(γ > 0) = 0.758, CI (−0.007, +0.014) → no premium. Prior sensitivity: informative N(1,0.1) vs diffuse N(0,10) → β changes by 0.002. Data dominates — large-sample convergence.

β_BTC posterior distributions — Year 1, Year 2+, Full sample · dashed = perfect tracking β = 1

Section 8 · Prior Sensitivity + MCMC

Data dominates priors. All chains converged.

PRIOR SENSITIVITY — β_BTC

Prior on βPost. mean95% CI
Informative N(1, 0.1)0.932(0.897, 0.966)
Diffuse N(0, 10)0.930(0.895, 0.965)

Difference: 0.002. With 597 observations, the likelihood overwhelms any reasonable prior — data dominates entirely.

Trace plots — dense caterpillar pattern = good mixing

MCMC DIAGNOSTICS — ALL 4 MODELS

ModelMax R-hatMin ESSStatus
Tracking (simple)1.0035,480✓ OK
Tracking (extended)1.0017,186✓ OK
Time-split Y11.0005,293✓ OK
Time-split Y2+1.0015,188✓ OK
R-hat ≤ 1.01 → all chains same distribution
Min ESS 5,188 — 13× the 400 threshold

ACF — autocorrelation near zero by lag 5

Conclusions

Five questions. Four answers. One surprise.

Q1 · Has IBIT's dominance eroded?

No — it became absolute. 34.8% in Year 1 → 99.7% in Year 2+. The market converged on BlackRock. Posteriors don't overlap.

Q2 · Who leads the second tier?

FBTC, decisively. 79.4% of days. ARKB sits right at fair share (P=0.628). BITB/HODL/BTCO are negligible.

Q3 · Positive expected return?

Uncertain. P(μ > 0) ≈ 0.63. ~19% annualised but 95% CI spans −34% to +49%. EMH not rejected.

Q4 · Rank ETFs by tracking quality?

Not possible from daily prices. Overnight BTC noise (136 bps/day) is 2,700× larger than fee differences (0.05 bps/day). All σ_TE posteriors overlap completely.

Q5 · Does IBIT faithfully track Bitcoin? ← The surprise

β = 0.932, P(β < 1) = 0.9999. A spot ETF holding only BTC should have β = 1.000 and R² > 0.99. It has neither. The 24/7 market vs 6.5h trading window creates a structural lag. No positive alpha. Consistent with EMH.

Discussion

Three limitations worth acknowledging

01 · DAILY DATA

Intraday bid-ask spreads and creation/redemption delays drive real tracking quality differences. Daily closing prices cannot resolve these — all σ_TE posteriors collapse to one number. Intraday data would give a richer picture.

02 · TWO-YEAR WINDOW

The regime-shift finding covers only the first two years of a genuinely new product class. The market is still settling. The three-tier structure (IBIT, FBTC, everyone else) may not be the long-run equilibrium.

03 · NORMALITY

The Normal-InvGamma model assumes iid Gaussian returns. Bitcoin has heavier tails. A Student-t likelihood would be more robust, but at the cost of the clean conjugate posteriors that made Sections 4–6 analytically tractable.

All code: quantmod · binom · LearnBayes · HDInterval · MCMCpack · brms · bayesplot · BayesFactor · invgamma · extraDistr

One winner.
Much to learn.
